q: The Crimean Offensive (1944) was a Soviet operation to recapture the Crimean peninsula from which occupying force?
e: The Crimean Offensive (8 April–12 May 1944) was a Soviet operation that rapidly expelled German and Romanian forces from Crimea, which had been under Axis occupation since 1942. The fall of Sevastopol on 9 May 1944 completed the liberation; about 130,000 Axis troops were killed or captured, and the remaining forces attempted a chaotic evacuation across the Black Sea.

q: The Battle of Arbedo (1422) was a defeat for Swiss Confederation forces at the hands of which Italian duchy's troops?
e: The Battle of Arbedo (30 June 1422) near Bellinzona saw Milanese cavalry under Carmagnola defeat a Swiss infantry force that had invaded the Duchy of Milan's Ticino region. The battle temporarily halted Swiss expansion southward into Italy and demonstrated that the previously near-invincible Swiss pike squares had vulnerabilities against coordinated cavalry.

q: The Siege of Güns (1532) is remembered because a small Hungarian garrison delayed the massive Ottoman army of which sultan?
e: The Siege of Güns (Kőszeg, August 1532) saw a tiny Croatian-Hungarian garrison of about 700–800 men under Nikola Jurišić hold off an Ottoman army of perhaps 120,000 under Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent for nearly three weeks. The delay prevented Suleiman from reaching Vienna before the campaigning season ended, and he withdrew without attempting the Austrian capital.

q: The Battle of Dogali (1887) was a catastrophic Italian colonial defeat at the hands of forces from which African empire?
e: The Battle of Dogali (26 January 1887) was fought near Massawa (modern Eritrea) when an Ethiopian force under Ras Alula ambushed and annihilated an Italian column of 548 soldiers under Lieutenant Colonel De Cristoforis. The disaster shocked Italy and foreshadowed the even greater Italian defeat at Adwa nine years later.

q: The Battle of Clastidium (222 BCE) was a Roman victory in which the consul Marcus Claudius Marcellus won the rare honor known as what?
e: The Battle of Clastidium (222 BCE) was fought during Rome's wars against the Gauls, in which consul Marcus Claudius Marcellus defeated the Insubrian Gauls near modern Casteggio. Marcellus killed the Gallic king Viridomarus in single combat and claimed the spolia opima — the rare honor of dedicating the enemy leader's armor to Jupiter — only the third Roman ever to do so.

q: The Battle of Wigan Lane (1651) was fought during the English Civil War, resulting in a Royalist defeat. The Royalist commander killed was a lieutenant-general of which foreign royal house?
e: The Battle of Wigan Lane (25 August 1651) was fought during Charles II's Scottish invasion of England, in which Parliamentarian forces under Robert Lilburne defeated a Royalist Scottish cavalry rearguard. Royalist lieutenant-general James Stanley, 7th Earl of Derby, was wounded here and later executed, making this one of the penultimate battles before Charles II's catastrophic defeat at Worcester.

q: The Battle of Ivry (1590) was a decisive Protestant Huguenot victory for which claimant to the French throne?
e: The Battle of Ivry (14 March 1590) was a decisive Huguenot victory during the French Wars of Religion, in which Henry of Navarre routed the Catholic League army under the Duke of Mayenne. Henry's rallying cry 'Follow my white plume!' became legendary. The victory allowed him to besiege Paris, and his subsequent conversion to Catholicism in 1593 finally ended the wars.

q: The Battle of Mansfield (1864) was a Confederate victory that halted which Union military campaign in Louisiana?
e: The Battle of Mansfield (8 April 1864), also known as the Battle of Sabine Crossroads, was a Confederate victory under Richard Taylor that decisively defeated Union General Nathaniel Banks's Red River Campaign. The Union force was routed with heavy losses in men and equipment, effectively ending Federal ambitions to penetrate into Texas via Louisiana.

q: The Siege of Calais (1346–1347) is associated with which famous act of surrender involving six volunteer hostages?
e: The Siege of Calais (1346–1347) ended when Edward III of England demanded that six leading citizens offer themselves as hostages with nooses around their necks and the city keys in hand. According to Froissart, Queen Philippa of Hainault begged for their lives and Edward spared them. Rodin immortalized the event in his famous sculpture 'The Burghers of Calais' (1889).

q: The Battle of Varese (1859) was a Risorgimento victory fought by whose irregular volunteer force?
e: The Battle of Varese (26 May 1859) was fought during the Second Italian War of Independence, in which Giuseppe Garibaldi's volunteer corps — the Cacciatori delle Alpi — defeated Austrian forces near Varese in Lombardy. The victory was part of a successful guerrilla campaign that distracted Austrian forces while the main Franco-Piedmontese army fought the decisive battles of Magenta and Solferino.

q: The Battle of Dessau Bridge (1626) was a defeat for Protestant forces during which major European war?
e: The Battle of Dessau Bridge (25 April 1626) was fought during the Danish Phase of the Thirty Years' War at Dessau-Roßlau, where Imperial general Albrecht von Wallenstein defeated a Protestant force under Ernest of Mansfeld attempting to cross the Elbe. The defeat severely weakened the Protestant military position in Germany and contributed to Denmark's eventual withdrawal from the war.

q: The Battle of Freiberg (1762) was the final major engagement of which long 18th-century conflict?
e: The Battle of Freiberg (29 October 1762) was the last significant battle of the Seven Years' War in the German theatre, in which Prussian forces under Prince Henry of Prussia defeated an Austrian-Imperial army near Freiberg in Saxony. The battle helped Prussia secure favorable peace terms and confirmed Frederick the Great's retention of Silesia in the Treaty of Hubertusburg.

q: The Battle of Hastenbeck (1757) was a French victory over the army of which German electorate?
e: The Battle of Hastenbeck (26 July 1757) was fought during the Seven Years' War near Hamelin, in which French forces under d'Estrées defeated the Allied army of George II's Hanover under the Duke of Cumberland. The defeat led to the Convention of Klosterzeven, by which Cumberland agreed to disband his army — a convention his father George II later repudiated.

q: The Siege of Ascalon (1153) allowed the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem to finally control which coastal route?
e: The Siege of Ascalon (January–August 1153) ended with the Crusaders capturing the last Egyptian (Fatimid) outpost in Palestine, closing off the main invasion route from Egypt. With Ascalon in Christian hands, the Kingdom of Jerusalem achieved its most strategically complete borders, though it remained vulnerable to attack from Syria.

q: The Battle of Fleurus (1794) was a French Republic victory that led to the permanent reoccupation of which region?
e: The Battle of Fleurus (26 June 1794) was a decisive French Republican victory during the War of the First Coalition, in which General Jourdan defeated the Austrian-Allied army under the Prince of Saxe-Coburg. Notably, a military observation balloon was used for the first time in battle. The victory led to the French conquest of the Austrian Netherlands (modern Belgium) and the Dutch Republic.

q: The Battle of Hundsfeld (1109) was a Polish victory that prevented the Holy Roman Empire from conquering which Polish-held region?
e: The Battle of Hundsfeld (24 August 1109), known in Poland as the Battle of Psie Pole ('Dogs' Field'), was fought near Wrocław where Duke Bolesław III of Poland defeated Holy Roman Emperor Henry V's invasion. The victory preserved Polish independence and Silesia, and Polish legend holds that the field got its name from the dogs feasting on German corpses after the battle.

q: The Battle of Jordanów (1939) was an early engagement in Nazi Germany's invasion of which country?
e: The Battle of Jordanów (1–3 September 1939) was fought in the Carpathian foothills during the opening days of the Nazi invasion of Poland, where Polish Podhale Rifle Brigade units fought a fierce delaying action against German armored forces. Polish cavalry and infantry briefly halted a German tank advance before being overwhelmed, demonstrating both the courage and the futility of Poland's defense against a mechanized enemy.

q: The Battle of Dahlen (1568) was part of which 16th-century religious and political revolt in the Low Countries?
e: The Battle of Dahlen (25 March 1568) was one of the earliest engagements of the Dutch Revolt (Eighty Years' War) against Spanish Habsburg rule. At Rheindahlen near Mönchengladbach, Spanish forces defeated a Dutch Protestant force, demonstrating the military dominance that Spain would maintain over the rebels for decades before the tide turned.

q: The First Battle of Villmergen (1656) was a Catholic cantonal victory in a Swiss religious civil war. What was this type of internal Swiss conflict called?
a: Kappel and Villmergen Wars (Swiss religious wars)
e: The First Battle of Villmergen (24 January 1656) was fought during the first Villmergen War, one of several Swiss internal religious wars between Catholic and Reformed Protestant cantons. The Catholic Forest Cantons defeated Zürich's Protestant forces at Villmergen, reasserting Catholic dominance within the Swiss Confederation — a dominance that was reversed in the Second Villmergen War of 1712.

q: The Battle of Magenta (1859) was a French-Piedmontese victory that caused Austria to evacuate which important Italian region?
e: The Battle of Magenta (4 June 1859) was a decisive Allied victory in the Second Italian War of Independence, fought near Magenta west of Milan. French forces under Marshal MacMahon broke through the Austrian line, forcing Austria to abandon Lombardy and allowing Napoléon III and Victor Emmanuel II to enter Milan in triumph. MacMahon was made Duke of Magenta for his role in the victory.

q: The Schlacht am Stoss (1405) was a Swiss-Appenzell peasant victory over the forces of which Austrian bishop?
e: The Schlacht am Stoss (17 June 1405) was fought at the Stoss Pass in Appenzell, where Appenzell peasants and their allies repulsed an invasion by forces loyal to the Habsburg-backed Abbot of St. Gallen and the Bishop of Constance. The decisive peasant victory established Appenzell's independence and became a foundational event in the canton's history.

q: The Battles of the Isonzo (1915–1917) were a series of costly Italian offensives against which empire?
e: The Battles of the Isonzo (1915–1917) were eleven major Italian offensives along the Isonzo River (modern Soča in Slovenia) against Austro-Hungarian defenses during the First World War. Despite enormous casualties on both sides — Italy suffered over 300,000 dead — Italy gained minimal territory until the catastrophic Austro-German breakthrough at Caporetto (the 12th Battle of the Isonzo) in October 1917.
s: The Battles of the Isonzo was a major historical military engagement. series of battles between Italy and Austria-Hungary during the First World War, primarily fought from 1915 to 1917

q: The Battle of Sörenberg (1380) was a conflict between two Swiss forces — the forest cantons against the troops of which city?
e: The Battle of Sörenberg (1380) was fought in the Entlebuch valley near Sörenberg, Switzerland, during a conflict between the forest cantons and the city of Lucerne over local dominance. The outcome eventually contributed to Lucerne integrating more closely with the Swiss Confederation rather than remaining an independent power in central Switzerland.

q: The Battle of Ragaz (1446) was fought during a conflict between the Swiss Confederation and the forces of which Habsburg-allied opponent?
e: The Battle of Ragaz (15 June 1446) was fought near Bad Ragaz in the Rhine Valley during the Old Zürich War, in which Swiss Confederation forces defeated an Austrian-allied army supporting the rights of the Toggenburg inheritance claimants. The battle reinforced Swiss military superiority in the region during this prolonged internal-external conflict.

q: Operation Masher (1966) was a large U.S. search-and-destroy mission during which conflict?
e: Operation Masher (later renamed Operation White Wing, January–March 1966) was one of the largest U.S. search-and-destroy operations of the Vietnam War, conducted in Bình Định Province. The operation involved the U.S. 1st Cavalry Division and South Vietnamese forces against North Vietnamese Army regulars and Viet Cong, and became controversial due to the high civilian casualties and President Johnson's objection to the original name 'Masher.'

q: The Siege of Odessa (1941) was a prolonged Romanian-German siege of the city, which was defended by which nation?
e: The Siege of Odessa (5 August–16 October 1941) was a 73-day defense by Soviet forces against the Romanian Third Army (with some German support) during Operation Barbarossa. Despite being surrounded, Soviet defenders — including Black Sea Fleet sailors — inflicted enormous casualties on the besieging forces before evacuating to Sevastopol when ordered to do so. Odessa was later awarded the title 'Hero City.'

q: The Battle of Kolubara (1914) was a Serbian counteroffensive victory over which invading power?
e: The Battle of Kolubara (3–15 December 1914) was one of the most remarkable reversals of the First World War, in which a Serbian army — half-starved, poorly equipped, and retreating — turned and decisively defeated a large Austro-Hungarian invasion force along the Kolubara River. The victory expelled Austrian forces from Serbia for nearly a year and boosted Allied morale.

q: The First Battle of Höchstädt (1703) was a Franco-Bavarian victory over Austrian forces during which major European war?
e: The First Battle of Höchstädt (20 September 1703) was fought during the War of the Spanish Succession near Höchstädt an der Donau, where a combined Franco-Bavarian force defeated an Austrian army under the Elector Palatine. However, the following year the Second Battle of Blenheim (Höchstädt) saw Marlborough and Eugene decisively reverse the situation and destroy the Franco-Bavarian army.

q: The Battle of Minden (1759) is famous for a celebrated infantry charge against cavalry during which conflict?
e: The Battle of Minden (1 August 1759) was a major Allied victory during the Seven Years' War, in which six British and Hanoverian infantry regiments famously advanced through artillery fire and routed the French cavalry, contrary to all military convention. British soldiers reportedly picked roses on their advance, giving rise to the Royal Hampshire Regiment's tradition of wearing roses on Minden Day.

q: Operation Menu (1969–1970) was a secret U.S. bombing campaign targeting North Vietnamese supply routes through which neutral country?
e: Operation Menu (March 1969–May 1970) was a covert U.S. Air Force bombing campaign of North Vietnamese Army and Viet Cong bases in neutral Cambodia, authorized by President Nixon and kept secret from Congress and the public. When revealed, it provoked enormous domestic controversy and contributed to the broader anti-war movement and the Watergate investigations.

q: The Battle of Bicocca (1522) saw Swiss mercenary infantry suffer heavy losses from which defensive innovation?
a: Field fortifications with massed arquebus fire
e: The Battle of Bicocca (27 April 1522) was a decisive Imperial-Spanish victory during the Italian Wars, in which Swiss infantry attacking across an open field were devastated by Spanish arquebusiers firing from prepared earthworks and a sunken road. The battle marked a turning point in military history — the decline of the previously dominant Swiss pike square against firearms and field fortifications.

q: The Battle of Olshanitsa (1527) was a decisive victory for Lithuania and Poland over forces of which eastern rival?
e: The Battle of Olshanitsa (27 January 1527) was fought near the Olshanitsa River in Ukraine, where Polish-Lithuanian forces inflicted a crushing defeat on a Crimean Tatar raiding army that had devastated the region. The victory was one of the most significant Polish-Lithuanian successes against the Tatars and temporarily reduced Crimean raiding pressure on the Commonwealth.

q: The Battle of Kappel (1531) ended with the death of which prominent Swiss Protestant reformer?
e: The Second Battle of Kappel (11 October 1531) was a Catholic Swiss cantonal victory over Protestant Zürich forces at Kappel am Albis. Among the dead was Huldrych Zwingli — the leader of the Swiss Reformation — who had accompanied the Zürich army as a chaplain. His death was a major blow to the Swiss Reformed movement, though his successor Heinrich Bullinger continued his work.

q: The Battle of Lautulae (315 BCE) was a rare Roman defeat during which ongoing conflict to control the Italian peninsula?
e: The Battle of Lautulae (315 BCE) was fought at Lautulae (a narrow coastal pass near Terracina) during the Second Samnite War, in which the Samnites won a rare victory over Roman forces. The defeat temporarily threatened Rome's grip on Latium and Campania, but Rome recovered and eventually prevailed in the Second Samnite War by 304 BCE.

q: The Battle of Elizabeth City (1862) was a Union naval victory during the American Civil War that secured control of which waterway?
e: The Battle of Elizabeth City (10 February 1862) was a Union naval victory following the capture of Roanoke Island, in which Commodore Goldsborough's gunboats destroyed the small Confederate Mosquito Fleet on the Pasquotank River. The victory gave the Union control of Albemarle Sound and the surrounding region, threatening Confederate supply lines in North Carolina.

q: The Battle of Wörth (1870) was a French defeat during the opening phase of which European war?
e: The Battle of Wörth (6 August 1870), also known as the Battle of Reichshoffen or Frœschwiller, was one of the largest battles of the Franco-Prussian War. Prussian Crown Prince Frederick William's army of 125,000 defeated Marshal MacMahon's French Army of Alsace, forcing it to retreat and opening Alsace to German occupation. The French cuirassier charges became legendary examples of gallantry against impossible odds.

q: The Battle of Ulrichen (1211) was a defensive victory that protected which Swiss valley from Savoyard invasion?
e: The Battle of Ulrichen (1211) was fought in the upper Valais (modern Goms district) near Ulrichen, Switzerland, where local Valais forces repulsed an invasion by Count Ulrich III of Arguel/Savoyard-affiliated troops. The victory preserved local autonomy in the upper Rhône valley and is considered an early example of the Swiss mountain communities' military self-defense.

q: The Second Battle of the Masurian Lakes (1915) was a German winter offensive that encircled elements of which Russian army?
e: The Second Battle of the Masurian Lakes (7–22 February 1915), also called the Winter Battle in Masuria, was a German offensive launched in the dead of winter from East Prussia against Russian forces in Poland. The German Eighth and Tenth Armies encircled the Russian XX Corps in the Augustów Forest, capturing most of it in sub-zero temperatures in one of the most complete German encirclements of the war.

q: The Siege of Madrid (1936–1939) during the Spanish Civil War was defended against which attacking force?
e: The Siege of Madrid (8 November 1936–28 March 1939) was one of the defining episodes of the Spanish Civil War, in which the Republican government's capital withstood a nearly three-year Nationalist siege. The defense became an international cause célèbre, with the International Brigades helping repulse early assaults. The city finally fell when internal Republican power struggles led to a coup that surrendered it to Franco.

q: The Battle of Winterthur (1799) was part of the French Revolutionary Wars' campaign in which central European region?
e: The Battle of Winterthur (27 May 1799) was fought during the War of the Second Coalition near Winterthur, Switzerland, in which Austrian forces defeated a French Republican army attempting to maintain control of Switzerland. The battle was part of a broader Allied offensive that temporarily expelled French forces from much of Switzerland, before Masséna's subsequent victory at the Second Battle of Zurich restored French dominance.

q: The Battle of Bezzecca (1866) was a Garibaldian victory that occurred during the Third Italian War of Independence fought alongside which broader European war?
e: The Battle of Bezzecca (21 July 1866) was fought during the Third Italian War of Independence, in which Giuseppe Garibaldi's volunteer corps defeated an Austrian force near Lake Garda. Italy fought alongside Prussia against Austria, and despite Italian defeats elsewhere (Custoza and Lissa), the Prussian victory compelled Austria to cede Venetia to Italy. Garibaldi famously responded to orders to halt with the telegram 'Obbedisco' (I obey).

q: The Battle of Borny-Colombey (1870) was the opening engagement of a French army's retreat toward which besieged fortress city?
e: The Battle of Borny-Colombey (14 August 1870) was fought during the Franco-Prussian War when Prussian forces intercepted Marshal Bazaine's French Army of the Rhine as it attempted to withdraw westward from Metz. The bloody engagement delayed the French retreat, allowing Prussian forces to outflank and eventually encircle the entire French Army of the Rhine at Metz, forcing its surrender in October 1870.

q: The Battle of Le Mans (1793) was a Republican victory against which major anti-Revolutionary uprising?
e: The Battle of Le Mans (12–13 December 1793) was a decisive Republican victory over the Vendée rebel army ('the Grand Catholic and Royal Army') that had crossed the Loire on the disastrous 'virée de Galerne' march. Republican forces under Kléber and Marceau inflicted enormous casualties on the rebels and effectively broke the organized Vendée army, though guerrilla resistance continued for years.

q: The Battle of Kashii (1615) took place during which decisive campaign that effectively unified Japan under the Tokugawa shogunate?
e: The Battle of Kashii (1615) was part of the Summer Campaign (夏の陣) — the final phase of the Siege of Osaka — in which Tokugawa Ieyasu's forces destroyed the Toyotomi clan and ended the last significant opposition to Tokugawa rule in Japan. The Toyotomi heir Toyotomi Hideyori and his mother Yodo-dono died in the fall of Osaka Castle, unifying Japan under the Edo shogunate.

q: The Battle of the Frontiers (August 1914) resulted in massive French casualties as France's offensive strategy — known by what name — failed disastrously?
e: The Battle of the Frontiers (14–25 August 1914) was a series of disastrous French offensive battles in Alsace-Lorraine, the Ardennes, and along the Sambre, fought under the aggressive offensive doctrine of Plan XVII. French forces charging with bayonets against entrenched German machine guns and artillery suffered over 300,000 casualties in two weeks — the bloodiest period of fighting in French military history — before the retreat that led to the 'Miracle of the Marne.'

q: The Battle of Mühldorf (1322) was a decisive victory that established which Habsburg ruler's claim to the Holy Roman Empire?
e: The Battle of Mühldorf (28 September 1322) was fought near Ampfing in Bavaria between rival claimants to the Holy Roman Empire: Louis IV of Bavaria and Frederick the Handsome of Austria. Louis decisively defeated Frederick, capturing him and holding him prisoner for years, effectively securing his position as Holy Roman Emperor despite continued papal opposition.

q: The Battle of Hohenfriedberg (1745) was a decisive Prussian victory during which major European conflict?
e: The Battle of Hohenfriedberg (4 June 1745) near Dobromierz (modern Poland) was a brilliant Prussian victory during the Second Silesian War, in which Frederick the Great launched a pre-dawn surprise attack and routed the combined Austrian-Saxon army. The Prussian cavalry's charge became one of the most celebrated actions of Frederick's career and is commemorated in the Hohenfriedberger Marsch.

q: The Stanleyville Mutinies (1966–1967) were military uprisings by Congolese soldiers who had previously served under which mercenary commander?
e: The Stanleyville Mutinies (1966–1967) were uprisings by Congolese soldiers loyal to ousted Prime Minister Moise Tshombe at Kisangani (formerly Stanleyville) against President Mobutu's government. The mutineers, supported by white mercenaries, briefly seized Kisangani before being suppressed by Mobutu's forces with external assistance. The events reflected the chaotic post-independence politics of the Congo.

q: The Konbaung–Hanthawaddy War (1752–1757) ended with the destruction of the Restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom, whose population was predominantly which ethnic group?
e: The Konbaung–Hanthawaddy War (1752–1757) ended with the Konbaung Dynasty under Alaungpaya completely destroying the Mon-dominated Restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom of southern Burma. The fall of the Mon capital Pegu in 1757 ended centuries of Mon political power in Southeast Asia, and many Mon people fled to Siam to escape subjugation by the new Burman-dominated Konbaung state.

q: The Kuwait–Nejd Border War (1919–1920) was a series of raids by Ikhwan warriors of which future Arabian state against Kuwaiti territory?
e: The Kuwait–Nejd Border War (1919–1920) saw Ikhwan religious warriors of Ibn Saud's Sultanate of Nejd conduct raids into Kuwait, which was a British protectorate. British air power and armored cars helped defend Kuwait, and the conflict helped define the eventual border between Kuwait and Saudi Arabia. It was part of the broader Ikhwan campaigns to expand Saudi influence across the Arabian Peninsula.
